title: What even is a variable?
instruction:
Remember in Chapter 1, you typed your name directly inside print()? Like `print("Alex")`?

What if you want to use your name in 10 different places? You would have to type it 10 times. And if you make a typo, you fix it 10 times.

There is a better way. **Store it once, use it everywhere.**

A variable is a labeled box. You put a value in it, give it a name, and from that point on you just use the name.

`name = "Alex"` — put "Alex" in a box, label it 'name'.

Run this and watch.
successMessage: See that? print(name) printed "Alex" — not the word 'name', but what name *holds*. The box metaphor is real. You labeled the box, put something in it, and then used the label.

title: Create your own
instruction:
Your turn! Create a variable called `name` and store YOUR name in it. Then print it.

Remember: text needs quotes. `name = Alex` crashes. `name = "Alex"` works.

The `=` sign here means *store this value* — not 'is equal to'. Think of it as an arrow: name ← "Alex".
starter: # TODO: create a variable called name with your name, then print it

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Your name, stored in a variable, printed to the screen. This is literally the foundation of every program ever written. Every app, every website, every game — variables everywhere.

title: Variables hold numbers too
instruction:
Variables are not just for text. They hold any kind of value — including numbers.

Here's the key difference:
- Text needs quotes: `name = "Alex"`
- Numbers do NOT: `age = 25` (not `age = "25"`)

Why does this matter? Because `25 + 5 = 30`, but `"25" + "5" = "255"`. Python treats them completely differently.

Create a variable `age` with your age and a variable `year` with the current year. Then print both.
starter: # TODO: create age and year variables, then print them

codeChecks.message: Create: age = 25 (use your actual age, no quotes!)
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Numbers without quotes — that's the rule. Text gets quotes, numbers don't. Get that muscle memory going now, it saves so many headaches later.

title: Variables can change
instruction:
Here is something that surprises some beginners: **a variable can change its value**. That is literally why they are called *variables*.

Run this. Score starts at 60, then jumps to 95. The variable updated — the old value is just gone.
successMessage: 60 then 95. That's reassignment — you just pointed the variable at a new value. In real programs, variables update constantly. A score changes, a username changes, a balance changes.

title: Use the current value to make a new one
instruction:
Here's a pattern you will use *all the time*:

`score = score + 10`

This sounds weird mathematically — 'score equals score plus 10?' But in programming, the right side runs first. Python takes the current value of score, adds 10, then stores the result back in score.

There is a starter score of 50. Add 10 to it twice, printing after each update.

Expected output: 50, then 60, then 70.
hint:
score = score + 10
print(score)
score = score + 10
print(score)
starter:
score = 50
print(score)
# TODO: add 10 to score twice, printing after each addition

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: 50 → 60 → 70. The variable carried its own running total. You will use this exact pattern for scores, counters, totals — basically everything in programming.

title: Two variables in one line
instruction:
Python lets you assign multiple variables at once — it's called tuple unpacking and it looks like this:

`first, last = "Alex", "Rivera"`

Python matches them left to right: first gets "Alex", last gets "Rivera".

Create `city` and `country` in one line, then print both.
starter: # TODO: assign city and country in one line, then print them

codeChecks.message: Assign both on one line: city, country = "City", "Country"
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Two variables in one line. Python unpacks from left to right, matching each name to each value. Clean and fast.

title: The Python swap trick
instruction:
In most languages, swapping two variables requires a third 'temporary' variable:

```
temp = a
a = b
b = temp
```

Python thinks that is too much typing. Here's the Python way:

`a, b = b, a`

One line. Python evaluates the entire right side first, then assigns. No temp variable needed.

Swap `first` and `last` the Python way.
starter:
first = "Alex"
last = "Rivera"
print(first, last)
# TODO: swap first and last in one line
print(first, last)

codeChecks.message: Swap them: first, last = last, first
Replace the TODO comment with your swap.

successMessage: Alex Rivera became Rivera Alex in one line. This is the kind of thing that makes Python devs smug at parties. It's elegant, and it actually works better than the temp-variable approach.

title: Rules for naming variables
instruction:
Variable names have rules. Break them and Python gets angry.

- Only letters, numbers, and underscores `_`
- Cannot start with a number
- No spaces (use `_` instead)
- No hyphens (those mean 'subtract')
- Python is case-sensitive: `score` and `Score` are different variables

The three names below are all illegal. Fix them all.
hint: 1stPlace → first_place, full name → full_name, user-score → user_score
starter:
# BUG: these variable names are invalid — fix all three
1stPlace = "Gold"
full name = "Alex Rivera"
user-score = 99

print(first_place)
print(full_name)
print(user_score)

codeChecks.message: Fix: user-score → user_score (hyphen is not allowed)

successMessage: Three illegal names, all fixed. The convention in Python is snake_case — lowercase words joined by underscores. first_name, user_score, total_price. You will write thousands of variable names in your career.

title: del — goodbye variable
instruction:
`del` removes a variable completely. After you delete it, trying to use it causes a `NameError` — Python has no idea what you are talking about.

It is like erasing the label on the box and throwing the box away.

Delete `temp` and then print a confirmation message.
starter:
temp = "I am temporary"
print(temp)
# TODO: delete temp and print "temp deleted"

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: del erases the variable from memory entirely. You won't use it every day, but it's good to know it exists — especially when working with large data you want to free up.

title: Build your profile
instruction:
Time to put it all together. No starter code — you are writing this one from scratch.

Create these four variables:
- `name` — your name
- `age` — your age (a number, no quotes!)
- `city` — your city
- `language` — `"Python"`

Then print all four, each on its own line.

You have done every single piece of this. You've got this.

codeChecks.message: Create a name variable with your name (in quotes).
Create an age variable (a number, no quotes).

successMessage: Four variables, four prints, all yours. Variables are in literally every program ever written. You now understand them better than most 'I know how to code' people do. Chapter 2 done.
